title: What is the real crux of the three loops article?
note: User asked for simple notes on the real crux and what Andrew Ng is suggesting.
synthesis: The article's crux is that AI agents speed up the inner engineering loop, so the bottleneck moves outward: from writing code, to steering product direction, to learning from real users. Andrew Ng is suggesting that engineers now need to think in nested loops: agentic coding loops for implementation, developer feedback loops for product judgment and context injection, and external feedback loops for market/user reality. The human role remains essential because humans hold context the AI lacks.

interaction.content: just make a few very simple notes here > what's the real crux/tldr of this article? what is he really getting at/suggesting?

Simple TLDR: coding agents make the code-building loop much faster, so the hard part moves up a level. The real work becomes deciding what to build, translating that into specs/evals, and using real user feedback to update the vision. Ng is saying product building now has three nested loops: the agent implements, the developer steers, and the outside world validates. His deeper suggestion is that engineers are becoming partial product managers because agents reduce implementation friction. But humans stay in the loop because they know user context the AI does not. Check-back: in your own words, what is the bottleneck shifting from and what is it shifting to?

title: Why does the cat costume story matter?
marker: Developer feedback loop / cat costumes example
note: User liked the cat costume story and asked how it relates to the three-loop framing.
synthesis: The cat costume example shows the developer feedback loop. The coding agent can execute the feature, but the human supplies context about the user: the child loves cats, costumes may motivate practice, and the parent flow needs to fit family use. This is Andrew Ng's point that humans still matter because they hold context the AI does not have.

interaction.content: i like the cat costume story - how does this relate?

The cat costume story is the cleanest example of the developer feedback loop. The agent can build whatever the spec says, but it does not know the real user: his daughter likes cats, unlockable costumes might make typing practice emotionally sticky, and the grown-up login flow has to match how a parent would actually steer learning. That is the human context advantage. It is not just taste as decoration; it changes what the product should be. Check-back: if the agent built a technically perfect typing app with no cat costumes and no parent flow, which loop failed: agentic coding, developer feedback, or external feedback? Why?
